title: The 10 Most Common GMP Audit Findings (and How to Fix Them)
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: The most common GMP audit findings and how to fix them: data integrity, deviations, CAPA, Annex 1 contamination control, supplier management and more.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a pristine pharmaceutical cleanroom corridor viewed at a slight angle, dominated by a cool teal and cyan palette layered over deep navy shadows, with a single subtle green accent glowing from a status indicator on stainless-steel equipment. Soft natural light spills from a frosted window, catching brushed-metal surfaces and a gowned operator blurred in the far background. Shallow depth of field keeps a clipboard and a sterile workbench in crisp focus while the corridor falls gently out of focus, conveying precision, calm authority and rigorous quality control. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the single most common GMP audit finding?
faqs.a: Data integrity and ALCOA+ failures consistently rank among the most cited findings in MHRA and EU inspections. Typical issues include shared logins, audit trails that are disabled or never reviewed, and the ability to overwrite original records. The remedy is unique user accounts, defined access privileges and periodic audit-trail review built into batch release.

faqs.q: How should we prepare for a GMP inspection?
faqs.a: Start with an honest, risk-based self-inspection that mirrors how a regulator actually looks at a site, then close the gaps with effective CAPA rather than quick corrections. A mock inspection by an independent contract auditor is one of the most reliable ways to surface findings before the MHRA does. Focus on your deviation backlog, audit-trail review and contamination control strategy first.

faqs.q: What does Annex 1 change for contamination control?
faqs.a: The revised EU GMP Annex 1 formalises the expectation of a holistic Contamination Control Strategy (CCS) that links premises, equipment, personnel and process, rather than relying on isolated controls. Inspectors now expect environmental monitoring to drive documented action on adverse trends, and aseptic technique and gowning to be properly qualified. Sterile and aseptic manufacturers without a documented CCS should treat this as a priority gap.
